description: Content that presents the same message or product pitch in both Arabic and English, with Arabic taking primary emphasis and English as secondary reinforcement. Creates cultural inclusivity while maintaining local authenticity.
structural_recipe: Lead with Arabic copy that establishes emotional connection and local flavor (use colloquialisms, mood words like 'مزاج رايق'). Include product description and promotional hook in Arabic. Follow with English translation or English-language repositioning of the same offer. Use identical visual product showcase. Include pricing/urgency elements ('الحين' / 'now') in Arabic. Emoji bridge between language sections to maintain visual continuity.
why_it_works: Saudi audiences respond to content in their native language for emotional engagement and trust-building, while English appeals to bilingual, cosmopolitan segments and signals international/premium positioning. Dual language signals inclusivity without alienating Arabic-first audiences. Creates perception of brand accessibility across demographics.
transplantation_caveats: Translation must be culturally accurate—literal translations of Arabic colloquialisms into English may lose impact
Language dominance matters: Arabic-first positioning is essential for authenticity in Saudi market
English-only audiences in Gulf may feel excluded if English section appears subordinate or poorly executed
Mixing casual Arabic (Khaliji dialect) with formal English may create tonal inconsistency
cultural_constraints: Arabic must be authentic Khaliji/Saudi dialect, not formal Modern Standard Arabic (MSA)
English should reflect premium/international positioning without condescension
Language order reflects cultural hierarchy: Arabic primacy acknowledges local identity
